title.title: Team Project
title.subtitle: Design a smart-contract business application — from problem statement through technical design to a deliverable PDF and presentation.

overview.heading: Project Overview
overview.subtitle: Identify a real-world problem, evaluate whether blockchain is the right tool, and design a smart-contract solution. Integrates all five courses of the Academy.
integrates: Blockchain Fundamentals — core concepts and trust models
Business Applications of Smart Contracts — design and decision framework
Blockchain Platforms — picking the right chain for your use case
Governance and Compliance — legal, regulatory, and on-chain governance
Project Management for Blockchain Initiatives — turning a design into a roadmap
overview.mindsetBody: think like blockchain entrepreneurs and consultants. Identify real opportunities, design viable solutions, navigate the trade-offs between technology, business, and regulation.

p1.heading: Problem Definition & Analysis
p1.subtitle: Before you design anything, prove there's a real problem worth solving — and that blockchain is the right tool for it.

cards.title: Problem Statement
items: What specific business problem are you solving? Be precise — name the actors, the activity, the friction.
What are the pain points in the current solution or process?
Who experiences these pain points, and how frequently?

cards.title: Current State Analysis
items: How is this problem addressed today (if at all)?
What inefficiencies, costs, or trust issues exist in the current approach?
Quantify wherever possible — cost, time, error rates, manual hours.

cards.title: Blockchain & SC Value Proposition
items: What value does a smart contract add vs. a traditional system?
Justify why blockchain is the right solution — or honestly flag the trade-offs if it's borderline.

cards.title: Stakeholder Mapping
items: Who are all the stakeholders? Who benefits — and by how much?
Who is disrupted or loses? How will they react?
What incentives align (or misalign) participant behavior?
Revenue model — transaction fees, subscription, token economics, or hybrid?
Competitive advantage and defensibility — why your team, why now?

valueFramework.lead: Run it through the decision framework: does this require

p2.heading: Technical Design
p2.subtitle: Pick a platform with a defensible rationale, sketch the smart-contract system, and identify what data lives where.

platform.title: Platform Selection
platform.item1: Which blockchain platform? Ethereum, Polygon, Arbitrum, Solana, Hyperledger Fabric, Corda…
platform.item2: — make the call and defend it.
platform.item3Em: transaction speed · cost · security · ecosystem maturity · use-case fit
platform.item4A: Don't just say "Ethereum" — explain why that's better than the next two alternatives for
platform.tipBody: reuse the platform-checklist interactive (Section 6) — your verdict is your answer here.

architecture.title: Smart Contract Architecture
architecture.item1A: High-level design of your dApp / contract system —
architecture.item2: What data does the contract need? What's on-chain vs oracle-fed vs off-chain?
architecture.item3: Trigger events, state variables, key functions — show the shape, you don't have to write the code.
architecture.item4: Security considerations: reentrancy? oracle dependencies? access control? front-running? Address explicitly.
architecture.bonusBody: a pseudocode contract or a real Solidity sketch in the appendix scores well.

p3.heading: Governance & Compliance
p3.subtitle: Smart contracts blend code and law. Spell out how decisions get made, which regulations apply, and what risks you're carrying.

cards.title: Governance Framework
cards.intro: Decide how the system steers itself once it's live.
points: How is the contract system governed day-to-day?
On-chain vs off-chain — which decisions live where?
DAOs, multisigs, or traditional governance — which fits?
Who can upgrade or pause the contract, and under what controls?

cards.title: Regulatory & Legal
cards.intro: Map the rules that apply before they bite you.
points: Jurisdiction — where do users and data live?
Applicable regulations: MiCA, securities law, AML / KYC, sector rules.
Legal viability today — what licenses or approvals are needed?
GDPR if you touch personal data — store hashes, not raw fields.

cards.title: Risk Assessment
cards.intro: Name what could go wrong — across three layers.
points: Technical · contract bugs, oracle failures, congestion, key loss.
Business · adoption, competition, regulatory shift, market timing.
Operational · team dependencies, custody, incident response.
For each row: likelihood × impact + a mitigation plan.

p4.heading: Implementation Roadmap
p4.subtitle: Turn the design into a phased plan. We don't need calendar dates — we need to know you've thought through what comes first, what depends on what, and how you'd measure success.

phases.desc: Smallest version that proves the value prop. One use case, narrow scope, manual fallbacks allowed.
Production-ready core. Audited contract, basic UI, real users, monitoring and incident response.
Feature-complete product. Integrations with adjacent systems, governance live, ecosystem hooks (oracles, indexers, marketplaces).

p4.eachPhaseBodyA: key milestones, dependencies between them, resources required (team composition + skills), and an
p4.eachPhaseBodyB: (small / medium / large — no calendar dates required).
p4.metricsIntro: How will you know each phase succeeded? Pick metrics that map directly to the pain points in Part 1.
metrics: Cost per transaction or per dispute
TVL or value flowing through the contract

evaluation.heading: Evaluation & Submission
evaluation.subtitle: How we grade the project — and exactly what to hand in.

criteria.label: Problem-Solution fit
criteria.sub: Is blockchain genuinely the right solution? Is the problem clearly defined?

criteria.label: Implementation Realism & Technical Soundness
criteria.sub: Is the roadmap achievable? Are risks properly assessed? Is the architecture sound?

criteria.label: Business Viability
criteria.sub: Clear path to value creation and sustainability. Bonus: business plan & GTM strategy.

criteria.label: Governance & Compliance
criteria.sub: Legal and governance considerations adequately addressed.

criteria.label: Stakeholder Analysis
criteria.sub: All stakeholders identified with clear benefit/cost analysis.

collaboration: Every team member contributes to research, writing, and presentation.
Document individual contributions in an appendix.
Apply project-management principles: assign roles, track progress, run internal reviews before submission.
evaluation.deadlineBody: June 10, 2026 — submissions close. Final presentations on June 9.

quiz.question: Your team is designing a smart contract for a crop-insurance payout: a sensor detects rainfall below a threshold, the contract automatically pays the farmer. The proposal earns full marks on Problem-Solution fit and Stakeholder Analysis, but the reviewer flags it as borderline. Which evaluation category most likely lost points — and what should you strengthen in the deliverable?
options: Presentation Quality (15%) — the deck wasn't polished enough; rewrite the executive summary.

Implementation Realism & Technical Soundness (20%) — the risk assessment should explicitly address oracle dependency on the rainfall sensor, the immutability problem if the sensor malfunctions, and the platform choice for reliable delivery.
Business Viability (20%) — the project needs a business plan and go-to-market strategy regardless of technical soundness.
Governance & Compliance (15%) — automated payouts always require explicit regulatory pre-approval before the project can be evaluated.
quiz.explanation: The Implementation Realism & Technical Soundness category (20%) covers risk assessment, architecture, and platform justification — exactly the gaps this design has. Specifically: (1) Oracle dependency — the rainfall sensor is a single point of failure; the deliverable must address how the project tolerates oracle compromise or downtime. (2) Immutability — if the sensor reports faulty data and the contract executes, the payout can't be reversed; what guard-rails or dispute windows exist? (3) Platform — does the chosen chain provide the data availability and finality this use case needs? Presentation Quality is about polish, Business Viability is about value-creation path, and Governance & Compliance — while important — wouldn't single-handedly flag a borderline verdict here. The right move is to strengthen Part 2 (Technical Design) and Part 3.3 (Risk Assessment).
